content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 265-266 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_001_page_001.jpg, 1PE_001_page_002.jpg

**1** From Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ — to God's chosen ones, living as strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, **2** chosen according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, set apart by the Spirit for obedience and for the sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ: May grace and peace overflow in your lives.

**3** All praise to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! In his great mercy, he gave us a completely new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, **4** and into an inheritance that can never be destroyed, never be stained, and never fade away — an inheritance kept safe in heaven for you, **5** who are being guarded by God's power through faith until the salvation that stands ready to be unveiled in the last days.

**6** This fills you with a tremendous joy, even though right now you may have to suffer through various trials for a little while. **7** The purpose of these trials is to prove that your faith is genuine — more precious than gold, which is tested by fire yet still perishes — so that your faith may result in praise and glory and honor when Jesus Christ is revealed. **8** Though you have never seen him, you love him. Though you do not see him now, you believe in him and are filled with a joy so glorious that words cannot express it, **9** because you are receiving the goal of your faith: the salvation of your very selves.

**10** The prophets who spoke of the grace intended for you searched and investigated this salvation with great care, **11** trying to determine the time and circumstances that the Spirit of Christ within them was pointing to when it testified in advance about the sufferings destined for Christ and the glories that would follow. **12** It was revealed to them that they were not serving themselves but you, when they spoke of the things that have now been announced to you by those who brought you the good news through the Holy Spirit sent from heaven — things that even angels long to look into.

**13** Therefore, prepare your minds for action. Stay clear-headed. Set your hope completely on the grace that will be brought to you when Jesus Christ is revealed. **14** As obedient children, do not let yourselves be shaped by the cravings you used to follow when you lived in ignorance. **15** Instead, just as the One who called you is holy, become holy yourselves in every aspect of your lives, **16** because it is written: "You shall be holy, because I am holy."

**17** And if you call on a Father who judges each person impartially according to what they have done, then live out your time as strangers here in reverent awe. **18** For you know that it was not with things that perish — silver or gold — that you were set free from the empty way of life handed down to you by your ancestors, **19** but with precious blood, like that of a lamb without defect or blemish — the blood of Christ. **20** He was chosen before the foundation of the world but was revealed in these final times for your sake. **21** Through him you are believers in God, who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, so that your faith and hope rest in God.

**22** Now that you have purified yourselves by obeying the truth, resulting in genuine love for your brothers and sisters, love one another deeply, from the heart. **23** For you have been born again — not from seed that decays, but from seed that is imperishable — through the living and enduring word of God. **24** For "all human life is like grass, and all its glory is like a wildflower. The grass withers and the flower falls away, **25** but the word of the Lord stands forever." And this is the word that was proclaimed to you as good news.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "living as strangers scattered throughout":** The Greek *parepideemois diasporas* (sojourners of the dispersion) carries a double weight — both physical displacement across the Roman provinces and a theological identity as people whose true homeland is elsewhere. The rendering "living as strangers scattered" captures both dimensions rather than choosing one.

**Verse 2 — "set apart by the Spirit for obedience and for the sprinkling of the blood":** Sinaiticus reads *en hagiasmo pneumatos eis hupakoeen kai rhantismon haimatos Ieesou Christou*. This dense Trinitarian formula (Father's foreknowledge, Spirit's sanctification, Son's blood) describes the full scope of what it means to be "chosen." The dynamic rendering unpacks the compressed Greek into accessible English while preserving the triad.

**Verse 3 — "a completely new birth into a living hope":** The Greek *anagenneesas heemas eis elpida zosan* (having begotten us again into a living hope) is a striking image — hope itself is described as alive, not static. The rendering emphasizes the radical newness of this birth.

**Verse 5 — "stands ready to be unveiled":** The Greek *hetoimen apokaluphthenai* (ready to be revealed) suggests salvation as something already complete, waiting behind a curtain. "Stands ready to be unveiled" captures this sense of imminence and completion.

**Verse 7 — "more precious than gold, which is tested by fire yet still perishes":** Sinaiticus reads *polutimoteron chrusiou tou apollumenou dia puros de dokimazomenou*. The point is paradoxical: gold is refined by fire but ultimately perishable; faith, also refined by fire, is far more valuable because it endures eternally.

**Verse 8 — "a joy so glorious that words cannot express it":** The Greek *agalliasthe chara aneklaleetoe kai dedoxasmenee* (you rejoice with inexpressible and glorified joy) piles up language to describe something that exceeds language. The dynamic rendering leans into this paradox.

**Verse 12 — "things that even angels long to look into":** The Greek *eis ha epithumousin angeloi parakupsai* uses *parakupsai* (to stoop down and peer in), an image of angels craning their necks to get a glimpse. The gospel is so magnificent that even heavenly beings are captivated by it.

**Verse 13 — "prepare your minds for action":** The Greek *anazosamenoi tas osphuas tees dianoias humon* (having girded up the loins of your mind) is a metaphor drawn from tucking robes into a belt before physical labor or travel. "Prepare your minds for action" conveys the urgency without the unfamiliar clothing reference.

**Verse 17 — "live out your time as strangers here in reverent awe":** The Greek *en phobo ton tees paroikias humon chronon anastrapheete* (conduct yourselves in fear during the time of your sojourning). The word *paroikia* (sojourning, from which we get "parish") reinforces the exile-identity theme begun in verse 1.

**Verse 18 — "set free from the empty way of life handed down to you by your ancestors":** The Greek *elutrootheete ek tees mataias humon anastrophees patroparadotou* (you were ransomed from your futile conduct inherited from your ancestors). The word *lutroo* (ransom/redeem) comes from the slave-market — the price of freeing a person from bondage. *Patroparadotou* (handed down from fathers) underscores that this emptiness was inherited, not chosen.

**Verse 20 — "chosen before the foundation of the world":** The Greek *proegnasmenou men pro kataboles kosmou* (foreknown before the foundation of the world) places Christ's redemptive role before creation itself. The Sinaiticus reading uses *proegnasmenou* (foreknown), emphasizing divine intentionality from eternity.

**Verse 23 — "not from seed that decays, but from seed that is imperishable":** The Greek *ouk ek sporas phthartees alla aphthartou* (not from perishable seed but imperishable) contrasts two kinds of origin. Human birth comes from mortal seed; spiritual rebirth comes from something that cannot decay — the living word of God.

**Verse 24-25 — Isaiah 40:6-8 quotation:** Peter quotes the Septuagint text of Isaiah. The Greek *pasa sarx hos chortos* (all flesh as grass) uses "flesh" in the Hebraic sense of mortal human existence. The rendering "all human life" captures this meaning. The quotation drives home the contrast between perishable human existence and the eternal word that brought about their new birth.

**Sinaiticus-specific note:** The Sinaiticus text of 1 Peter 1 is well-preserved across pages 265-266. The chapter begins in the third column of page 265 (immediately following James) and concludes in the second column of page 266. The manuscript shows the typical scriptio continua format with nomina sacra abbreviations for sacred names. Notable Sinaiticus readings include *elpida zosan* (living hope) in verse 3 and the inclusion of *dia pneumatos hagiou* (through the Holy Spirit) in verse 12, consistent with the broader manuscript tradition for this passage.
